1.synthesis: It is actually reported that there is sexual immorality among them—and such immorality as is not even among pagans: a man has his father's wife (1 Corinthians 5:1). They are arrogant rather than mourning—so that he who has done this would be removed from among them. Though absent in body but present in spirit, Paul has already pronounced judgment on the man who did this (1 Corinthians 5:2–3). When they are assembled in the name of the Lord Jesus and his spirit is present, with the power of the Lord Jesus, they are to deliver this man to Satan for the destruction of the flesh, so that his spirit may be saved in the day of the Lord (1 Corinthians 5:4–5). Their boasting is not good. Do they not know that a little leaven leavens the whole lump? Cleanse out the old leaven that they may be a new lump—as they really are unleavened. For Christ, our Passover lamb, has been sacrificed (1 Corinthians 5:6–7). Let them keep the feast not with the old leaven of malice and evil, but with the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth (1 Corinthians 5:8).
1.historical_context: The relationship in question (a man living with his step-mother) was forbidden by both Jewish law (Leviticus 18:8) and Roman law (incest with a step-mother was a capital offense). Paul's shock that this is 'not even among pagans' is significant: the pagan world regarded this relationship as taboo. The Corinthians' arrogant tolerance may have reflected a misapplication of the 'everything is permissible' slogan (cf. 1 Corinthians 6:12) or a form of proto-Gnostic indifference to bodily behavior. The 'deliver to Satan' formula (v.5) is the NT's most explicit disciplinary sanction: exclusion from the protective community of Christ, which was understood to expose the person to the domain of the adversary. The redemptive purpose—'so that his spirit may be saved in the day of the Lord'—specifies the aim: discipline that aims at restoration, not elimination. The Passover typology (vv.6–8) applies the Exodus cleansing command to the church's situation: as Israel removed leaven before Passover, so the community must remove the corrupting element.
1.literary_structure: The passage moves from denunciation (vv.1–2) to the disciplinary action prescribed (vv.3–5) to the theological rationale (vv.6–8). The Passover typology is the theological heart: it identifies Christ as the Passover lamb (already sacrificed) and the community as the unleavened batch that must live consistently with its paschal identity. The leaven metaphor (a little leaven leavens the whole, v.6) grounds the disciplinary action in a communal principle: moral compromise that is tolerated spreads through the entire community.
1.theological_application: The Corinthians' arrogant tolerance of gross moral failure in their midst is the inverse of the problem Paul addressed in Romans 14–15 (judging over indifferent matters). Here the matter is not indifferent but a clear violation of moral boundaries recognized even by pagans. The church must distinguish between the judgment of brothers over secondary matters (which Paul forbids) and the protection of the community from corrupting moral compromise (which Paul requires). The redemptive purpose of the 'deliver to Satan' discipline is crucial: this is not punitive expulsion but restorative shock therapy—the aim is the person's ultimate salvation, not their permanent exclusion. The Passover typology grounds communal purity in christological identity: the church has already been cleansed by the sacrifice of the true Passover lamb; to tolerate sexual immorality in its midst is to live inconsistently with what it already is in Christ. Community integrity and personal ethics are not optional extras but consequences of paschal identity.
1.christology: 'Christ, our Passover lamb, has been sacrificed' (v.7) is one of Paul's most concentrated sacrificial christological statements. The identification of Jesus with the Passover lamb connects his death to the foundational event of Israel's liberation: as the lamb's blood protected Israel from the destroyer and enabled their exodus, so Christ's blood protects those who belong to him and enables their exodus from slavery to sin. The ethical implication is immediate: those who have been liberated by the true Passover must live as a liberated people—not by tolerating the moral compromise that belongs to the old existence but by becoming in practice the 'new lump' that they already are in Christ. The feast of sincerity and truth (v.8) is the ongoing celebration of the paschal liberation, lived out in community purity that corresponds to the lamb's sacrifice.

9.synthesis: Paul wrote to them in his letter not to associate with sexually immoral people—not meaning the sexually immoral of this world, or the greedy and swindlers, or idolaters, since then they would need to go out of the world (1 Corinthians 5:9–10). But now he writes: do not associate with anyone who bears the name of brother if he is sexually immoral or greedy or an idolater or a reviler or a drunkard or a swindler—not even to eat with such a one (1 Corinthians 5:11). For what does he have to do with judging outsiders? Is it not those inside the church whom they are to judge? God judges those outside. 'Purge the evil person from among you' (1 Corinthians 5:12–13).
9.historical_context: Paul clarifies a misunderstanding from a previous letter (now lost) that had apparently been read as requiring withdrawal from the world entirely. His correction is socially crucial: Christians are not a separatist sect required to avoid all contact with immoral people in society (they would have to 'leave the world') but are called to maintain the moral distinctiveness of the covenant community by not accepting gross moral compromise from those who claim to be believers. The distinction between judging 'insiders' and 'outsiders' is theologically significant: the church has no mandate to impose its moral standards on those outside the community of Christ; its disciplinary authority extends only to those within the covenant. The Deuteronomy citation ('purge the evil person from among you,' Deuteronomy 17:7) applies the OT community-protection command to the new covenant assembly.
9.literary_structure: The passage resolves a practical misunderstanding while establishing a principle: the church's judgment operates differently toward insiders and outsiders. Outsiders are God's to judge; insiders are the community's responsibility to discipline. The expansion of the category of disciplinable sins beyond sexual immorality (to include greed, idolatry, reviling, drunkenness, swindling, v.11) demonstrates that the concern is the community's overall moral integrity, not merely one spectacular case. The 'not even to eat with such a one' prohibition likely refers to the Lord's Supper, the communal meal that was the central expression of shared fellowship—exclusion from table fellowship was the concrete form of the discipline.
9.theological_application: The insiders/outsiders distinction is one of the most practically important principles in Paul's ecclesiology. The church is called to be distinct—a community with genuine moral standards that correspond to the gospel—but this distinctiveness is expressed through internal accountability, not external condemnation of those who have made no covenant commitment. The church loses its witness when it does the opposite: imposing moral standards on the surrounding culture while tolerating the same behaviors among its own members. The list of disciplinable behaviors (sexual immorality, greed, idolatry, reviling, drunkenness, swindling) is notable for including economic sins (greed, swindling) alongside sexual ones—suggesting that the community's integrity encompasses the full range of behavior, not just the sins that attract the most rhetorical energy. The table-fellowship exclusion models a graduated social response rather than a single dramatic expulsion: the person is not to be treated as an enemy but as someone whose fellowship-status has changed until repentance occurs.
9.christology: The community's judgment of its own members is exercised 'in the name of the Lord Jesus' (1 Corinthians 5:4)—the disciplinary authority derives from him, not from apostolic preference or community consensus. The goal of the entire process is the person's ultimate salvation on 'the day of the Lord' (v.5), which is the day of Christ's return. The church's disciplinary authority is therefore always eschatological in orientation: it operates in anticipation of the final judgment while seeking to avoid that judgment by means of redemptive correction now. God's judgment of those outside (v.13) does not make the church passive about the world—evangelism continues—but it properly locates the church's disciplinary authority within its own covenant community rather than directing it as moralistic condemnation toward those outside.
